claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me how many children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm looking to claim for my two children. I have Alex, who's 4 years old, and Blake, who's 14.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you're looking to claim Child Benefit for Alex (age 4) and Blake (age 14).

Now, could you please confirm whether you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you for confirming that.

Do both Alex and Blake live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Regarding Alex, who doesn't live with you – could you tell me a bit more about their living situation? For example:

- Who does Alex live with (e.g., a relative, friend, or are they in care)?
- Do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep (such as food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation costs)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent. Yes, I do contribute financially - I help out with things like clothes, food, and other expenses they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Regarding your financial contributions for Alex – would you say the amount you contribute is at least equal to the Child Benefit rate (currently £25.60 per week for a first child, or £16.95 per week for additional children)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute more than that. I give £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One more question about Alex – is there anyone else (such as Alex's other parent) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful to know.

Now, just a couple of quick questions about Blake, who lives with you:

1. Is Blake in any form of local authority care, hospital, or residential accommodation?
2. Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in any care, hospital, or residential accommodation - they live at home with me. And no, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both children.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

---

## Overall Result: You appear to be eligible for Child Benefit for both children 🎉

### Alex (age 4) ✅ Eligible
Although Alex doesn't live with you, you contribute £50 per week towards their upkeep. This exceeds the Child Benefit rate, which means you qualify as being "responsible" for Alex under the eligibility rules. Since no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you would be eligible to claim.

### Blake (age 14) ✅ Eligible
Blake lives with you in the UK, is under 16, is not in care or hospital, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria.

---

### Important Notes:
- **Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child.** If Alex's other parent decides to claim in the future, you would need to agree between yourselves who should receive it. If you cannot agree, HMRC will decide.
- Be aware that if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay a High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

If you'd like to proceed, you can make a claim for Child Benefit through the official government channels. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old (under 16) and although Alex doesn't live with you, you contribute £50 per week towards their upkeep, which is more than the Child Benefit rate (£25.60 for a first child or £16.95 for additional children). Under the rules, you are normally responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Since no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you would be eligible to claim.

Blake is 14 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, is not in care, hospital, or residential accommodation, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You live in the UK and are responsible for both children. For Blake, you meet the standard criteria as Blake lives with you and is under 16. For Alex, although they live with their other parent, you contribute more than the Child Benefit rate towards their upkeep (£50 per week), which makes you eligible. Since no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for either child, you would be eligible to claim for both Alex and Blake.

Under the rules, you are normally responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. Since no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you would be eligible to claim.

Blake is 14 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, is not in care, hospital, or residential accommodation, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Blake.

For Alex, although they live with their other parent, you contribute more than the Child Benefit rate towards their upkeep (£50 per week), which makes you eligible. Since no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for either child, you would be eligible to claim for both Alex and Blake.
